message.content:
You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer A) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10, latest commit). Confirm the fix is BOTH appropriate (correct root cause, sound approach, right layer) AND complete (no residual gap; regression test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted). Be skeptical; find holes.

FINDING P10-PII-2:
The machine-hostname rule (scripts/capture_transcripts.py) used re.escape(b)+r"(?:\.local)?\b" with NO leading boundary → substring over-redaction (host 'host' redacted inside 'localhost'). Fix: prepend r"\b". VERIFY: with a derived hostname 'host', _scrub does NOT redact 'localhost'/'localhostname'/'rehosting' but DOES redact a bare 'host' token / 'host.local'. Test: test_scrub_hostname_does_not_over_redact_as_substring. Confirm the distinctive-hostname case ([REDACTED]) still redacts.

Read the changed files and run what you can (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q <path>; "python -m pytest"; gh for CI). Judge ONLY P10-PII-2. CONFIRMED only if appropriate AND complete; CONCERN if a real residual gap; REJECTED if wrong/non-discriminating. List concrete concerns (empty if none).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

230	
231	        for arg in ("LocalHostName", "ComputerName"):
232	            out = subprocess.run(["scutil", "--get", arg], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
233	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():

235	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - scutil absent (non-macOS) must never break capture
236	        pass
237	    extra = [h.strip() for h in os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", "").split(",") if h.strip()]
238	
239	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
240	    seen: set[str] = set()
241	
242	    def _base(name: str) -> str:
243	        n = name.strip()
244	        return n[: -len(".local")] if n.lower().endswith(".local") else n
245	
246	    for name in machine:  # machine hostname → redact BARE + .local (distinctive identity)
247	        b = _base(name)
248	        if len(b) < 4 or b.lower() in seen:
249	            continue
250	        seen.add(b.lower())
251	        # Leading \b (#P10-PII-2): without it the bare hostname matches as a SUBSTRING, so a short/
252	        # common derived hostname (e.g. "host" on a CI runner) would corrupt legit content — "host"
253	        # inside "localhost" → "local[REDACTED]". The boundary requires a token start.
254	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\b" + re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE))
255	    for name in extra:  # supplied namespace hosts → redact ONLY the .local FQDN form
256	        b = _base(name)
257	        if len(b) < 4 or ("local:" + b.lower()) in seen:
258	            continue
259	        seen.add("local:" + b.lower())
260	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"\.local\b", re.IGNORECASE))
261	    return pats

270	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
271	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
272	        if pat.groups == 2:
273	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
274	        elif pat.groups == 1:
275	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
276	        else:
277	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
278	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
279	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
280	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
281	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
282	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
283	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
284	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
285	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
286	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
287	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
288	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
289	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
290	    # Redact the machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-PII-1) — the production node's hostname and
291	    # internal *.local hosts that no other rule covers.
292	    for pat in _HOSTNAME_RES:
293	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
294	    return text

199	def test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames(monkeypatch):
200	    # #P9-PII-1: the production node's hostname and internal *.local hosts are operator/infra PII
201	    # that no email/username/handle rule covered. The machine hostname is redacted bare + .local;
202	    # an ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS namespace host has its .local form redacted while the bare project token
203	    # (which appears in legit paths/repo refs) is preserved, and unrelated "*.local" is untouched.
204	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", "hc-proj")
205	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_host")
206	    # force a deterministic machine hostname regardless of the test host
207	    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.socket, "gethostname", lambda: "buildbox-7")
208	    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.platform, "node", lambda: "buildbox-7")
209	    cap._HOSTNAME_RES = cap._hostname_patterns()
210	    out = cap._scrub(
211	        "ran on buildbox-7.local (bare buildbox-7); ns https://hc-proj.local/prov#; "
212	        "repo hc-proj/app and settings.local.json"
213	    )
214	    assert "buildbox-7" not in out  # machine hostname redacted (bare + .local)
215	    assert "hc-proj.local" not in out  # namespace host .local form redacted
216	    assert "hc-proj/app" in out  # bare project token preserved (not over-redacted)
217	    assert "settings.local.json" in out  # unrelated *.local untouched (no blanket rule)
218	
219	
220	def test_scrub_redacts_private_ips_keeps_loopback_and_public():
221	    # #P10-PII-1: captured ifconfig/route/lsof output leaks the operator's LAN subnet/gateway/host
222	    # IP (RFC1918). Redact 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16 — including a form glued to a JSON-\n — while
223	    # KEEPING loopback (127.x), 0.0.0.0, and public IPs (remote API endpoints, not operator PII).
224	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_ip")
225	    out = cap._scrub(
226	        "gw [REDACTED] host [REDACTED] ten [REDACTED] priv [REDACTED] "
227	        "loop 127.0.0.1 any 0.0.0.0 pub 160.79.104.10 glued:\\n[REDACTED]"
228	    )
229	    for priv in ("[REDACTED]", "[REDACTED]", "[REDACTED]", "[REDACTED]", "[REDACTED]"):
230	        assert priv not in out, f"private IP {priv} leaked"
231	    assert "127.0.0.1" in out and "0.0.0.0" in out and "160.79.104.10" in out  # kept
232	
233	
234	def test_scrub_hostname_does_not_over_redact_as_substring(monkeypatch):
235	    # #P10-PII-2: the machine-hostname rule has a leading \b so a short/common hostname does not
236	    # match as a substring of legitimate content (e.g. host "host" must NOT redact "localhost").
237	    monkeypatch.delenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", raising=False)
238	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_hostsub")
239	    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.socket, "gethostname", lambda: "host")
240	    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.platform, "node", lambda: "host")
241	    cap._HOSTNAME_RES = cap._hostname_patterns()
242	    out = cap._scrub("connect to localhost; the localhostname var; rehosting the service")
243	    # leading \b prevents the SUBSTRING matches that were the defect: 'host' must not fire inside
244	    # 'localhost', 'localhostname', or 'rehosting'.
245	    assert "localhost" in out and "localhostname" in out and "rehosting" in out, (
246	        "over-redacted 'host' as a substring (#P10-PII-2)"
247	    )

251	    # #P9-PII-1 confirm-concern: the namespace host ([REDACTED]) only redacts when ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS
252	    # names it; the machine hostname auto-derives but this does not. The pre-commit hook must set
253	    # ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS before running capture, else every commit re-introduces [REDACTED].

258	    assert "ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS" in pre, "ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS set after capture (too late)"

261	def test_example_uses_no_hardcoded_hmac_secret():
262	    # #F-048: the example must not carry a shippable, publicly-known HMAC secret a user could copy
263	    # into production. It derives a fresh random secret per run instead.
264	    src = (_ROOT / "examples" / "minimal_study.py").read_text(encoding="utf-8")
265	    assert b"example-study-secret-key".decode() not in src  # the stale weak literal is gone
266	    assert "os.urandom(32)" in src  # the fresh-per-run secret is in place

content.text: I notice a subtlety. The test asserts `host` does NOT redact inside `localhost`/`localhostname`/`rehosting`, but it does NOT assert that a bare `host` token or `host.local` DOES still get redacted. The finding asks to confirm both the negative AND positive cases. Let me check whether `host` (len 4) even gets a pattern (len >= 4 check passes), and verify the positive case manually. Let me run the tests and do a discrimination check.

Actually the persistence is because my inline test's second regeneration picked up real scutil output. That's a test-harness artifact, not a code issue. The key results are clear:
- Patterns now have leading `\b`
- Bare `host` token → redacted (positive)
- `host.local` → redacted (positive)

content.text: The test PASSED even with the fix reverted. That's a non-discrimination concern. But wait — `_load` in the test re-imports the module fresh and calls `_hostname_patterns()` after monkeypatching. Let me check how `_load` works and whether the sed actually affected the loaded module. The test uses `cap._HOSTNAME_RES = cap._hostname_patterns()` so it regenerates from the edited source. Let me re-verify the revert was actually in place when the test ran — the restore happened in the same command. Let me redo carefully.

19	    assert spec and spec.loader
20	    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
21	    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
22	    return mod

39	    # Discriminating: intercept the module's open() and capture the file handle; after main()
40	    # returns, assert it is .closed. A bare json.load(open(...)) leaves the handle open (closed
41	    # only later by GC), so this fails when the context-manager fix is reverted.

content.text: `_load` reads from disk fresh each time, so the sed revert WAS in effect. Yet the substring test passed. Let me investigate why — without the leading `\b`, why doesn't `host` match inside `localhost`? Because of the TRAILING `(?:\.local)?\b`. In `localhost`, the substring `host` is followed by end-of-word boundary... `localhost` — `host` is at the end, followed by `;` so `\b` matches. So without leading `\b`, `re.sub` would find `host` inside `localhost` and redact it. Let me directly test the reverted pattern.

input.command:

content.text:
File restored to the fixed state (git diff clean). 

My findings are conclusive. The fix itself is correct and complete: prepending `\b` to the machine-hostname pattern correctly prevents substring over-redaction while still redacting bare tokens and `.local` forms, including the distinctive `[REDACTED]`. I verified all positive and negative cases directly against the regex.

However, the regression test `test_scrub_hostname_does_not_over_redact_as_substring` does NOT discriminate: it passes even when the fix is reverted. The reason is that with the buggy (no-leading-`\b`) pattern, the input string is corrupted to `local[REDACTED]; the localhostname var; rehosting`, but the assertion `assert "localhost" in out` is satisfied by the substring `localhost` inside the still-intact `localhostname` token. So the three substring assertions mutually mask the actual corruption of the standalone `localhost` token. The test gives false assurance.

input.rationale:
The code fix for P10-PII-2 is correct, appropriate, and complete at the right layer: scripts/capture_transcripts.py line 254 now compiles the machine-hostname pattern as r"\b" + re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b". I verified the regex behavior directly: with derived hostname "host", the fixed pattern does NOT touch "localhost"/"localhostname"/"rehosting" yet still redacts a bare "host" token and "host.local"; and the distinctive hostname "[REDACTED]" still redacts both bare and .local forms. Root cause (missing leading boundary causing substring over-redaction) is correctly addressed in the pattern-construction layer, consistent with how the namespace (.local-only) and other rules are built.

BUT the regression test does NOT discriminate. I reverted the fix (removed the leading \b) and ran tests/test_scripts.py::test_scrub_hostname_does_not_over_redact_as_substring — it still PASSED. I traced why: with the buggy pattern the scrub output is 'connect to local[REDACTED]; the localhostname var; rehosting the service' — the standalone 'localhost' token IS corrupted to 'local[REDACTED]', exactly the defect. However the test asserts `"localhost" in out`, which is satisfied by the substring 'localhost' contained inside the still-intact 'localhostname' token. So the assertions mutually mask each other and the test gives a false pass on the unfixed code. A regression test that does not fail when the fix is reverted does not lock in the fix.
concerns: NON-DISCRIMINATING TEST: test_scrub_hostname_does_not_over_redact_as_substring passes even with the fix reverted. Proof: reverted pattern yields out='connect to local[REDACTED]; the localhostname var; rehosting the service'; the assertion `"localhost" in out` is True only because 'localhost' is a substring of the untouched 'localhostname', masking that the standalone 'localhost' token was corrupted to 'local[REDACTED]'. The test must assert on the exact intact token, e.g. `"to localhost;" in out` or `out.count("[REDACTED]") == 0` (with the bare-host positive case in a separate input), so it fails when reverted.

MISSING POSITIVE ASSERTIONS in the new test: it only checks the negative (no over-redaction) cases. The finding asks to confirm a bare 'host' token and 'host.local' STILL redact. The new substring test contains no positive-redaction assertion (the existing test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames covers a distinctive name but not the short/common 'host' case). I verified the positive cases manually and they pass, but they are not pinned by a test.

The fix to scripts/capture_transcripts.py (line 254) is correct, appropriate, and complete — but the regression test test_scrub_hostname_does_not_over_redact_as_substring in tests/test_scripts.py is non-discriminating: it passes even when the fix is reverted, because the assertion `"localhost" in out` is satisfied by the substring inside the untouched `localhostname` token, masking that the standalone `localhost` was corrupted to `local[REDACTED]`.
